content: Jeevamrutha is a fermented microbial culture used as a soil inoculant in Zero Budget Natural Farming (ZBNF). It is NOT a fertiliser — it is a concentrated source of beneficial soil microorganisms. Preparation: Mix 10 kg fresh local cow dung, 10 litres cow urine, 2 kg jaggery (belladakayi), 2 kg pulse flour (any dal atta), and a handful of undisturbed forest soil in a 200-litre barrel of water. Stir clockwise for 2 minutes. Cover with a cloth (not airtight) and ferment for 48 hours in shade. Must be used within 7 days of preparation. Application: Dilute and apply through irrigation at 200 litres per acre every 15 days. The forest soil provides the initial microbial seed culture containing Azotobacter, Phosphobacteria, Pseudomonas, and Lactobacillus species.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: Jeevamrutha works because of the microbiology of the cow's digestive system. A cow's rumen contains over 3 billion beneficial bacteria per millilitre, including nitrogen-fixing Azotobacter, phosphate-solubilizing bacteria, and cellulose-decomposing organisms. When cow dung is mixed with jaggery (carbon source for bacterial growth) and pulse flour (nitrogen source), and incubated for 48 hours, these bacteria multiply rapidly. The resulting culture, when applied to soil, colonizes the root zone (rhizosphere) and performs three critical functions: (1) fixes atmospheric nitrogen into plant-available form, (2) solubilizes bound phosphorus in soil, and (3) produces growth hormones (IAA, gibberellins) that stimulate root development. This is not superstition — it is applied microbiology. The same principle underlies commercial biofertilisers, but Jeevamrutha is made on-farm at zero cost.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: Bijamrutha (Beejamrutha) is an organic seed treatment used before sowing to protect seeds from soil-borne pathogens and to inoculate them with beneficial microorganisms. Preparation: Take 5 kg fresh local cow dung, 5 litres cow urine, 50 grams lime powder (calcium hydroxide), and a handful of undisturbed soil from beneath a large tree. Mix thoroughly in 20 litres of water. Allow the mixture to ferment overnight (12 hours). Application: Soak seeds in this solution for 20-30 minutes before sowing. For tubers and rhizomes, dip them for 10-15 minutes. The cow dung provides beneficial bacteria, the urine acts as a natural fungicide (contains urea, allantoin, and hippuric acid), and the lime creates an alkaline barrier against fungal pathogens like Fusarium and Pythium.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: Gau Krupa Amrutha is an enhanced version of Jeevamrutha with additional herbal ingredients for improved soil biological activity. Preparation: Start with the base Jeevamrutha recipe (10 kg cow dung, 10L cow urine, 2 kg jaggery, 2 kg pulse flour, handful of forest soil in 200L water). Add 1 kg neem leaf paste, 500g Calotropis (Ekka) leaf paste, and 250g turmeric powder. These herbal additions serve specific purposes: neem provides natural pest deterrent compounds (azadirachtin), Calotropis provides antifungal properties, and turmeric acts as an antibacterial agent. Ferment for 72 hours (longer than regular Jeevamrutha) to allow the herbal compounds to integrate with the microbial culture. Use within 5 days. Apply at 200 litres per acre.
source_doc: Traditional Karnataka Organic Practices

content: Kunapa Jala (Vegetarian Version) is a liquid organic fertiliser rich in nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium derived from plant-based oil cakes. This non-animal version avoids the traditional practice of using animal carcasses. Preparation: Mix 5 kg neem cake, 3 kg castor cake, 2 kg groundnut cake, 2 kg jaggery, and 5 kg cow dung in a 200-litre barrel. Fill with water, stir well, and cover loosely. Allow to ferment for 30 days, stirring every 3 days. The fermentation breaks down complex proteins in the oil cakes into amino acids and releases bound nutrients. The resulting liquid is a complete plant nutrition source. Application: Dilute 1 part Kunapa Jala in 10 parts water. Apply through irrigation or as a foliar spray. Rich in nitrogen (from protein breakdown) and micro-nutrients.
source_doc: Vruksha Ayurveda - Surapala

content: Panchagavya is a traditional bio-stimulant made from five products of the cow (pancha = five, gavya = cow-derived). Ingredients: (1) Fresh cow dung — 7 kg, (2) Cow urine — 3 litres, (3) Cow milk — 2 litres, (4) Cow curd/yoghurt — 2 litres, (5) Cow ghee — 1 litre. Additionally add: 3 litres sugarcane juice (or 500g jaggery dissolved in 3L water), 12 ripe bananas (mashed), and 3 litres tender coconut water. Preparation: Day 1 — Mix cow dung and ghee, stir twice daily. Day 3 — Add cow urine. Day 5 — Add milk, curd, sugarcane juice, banana, and coconut water. Stir twice daily for 21 days total. The resulting Panchagavya contains beneficial bacteria, fungi (Trichoderma, Saccharomyces), growth hormones (IAA, GA), and micronutrients. Application: 3% spray (3L Panchagavya in 100L water) as foliar application every 15 days.
source_doc: Tamil Nadu Agricultural University - Organic Farming Guide

content: Vermicompost Production from Cow Dung: Vermicompost is produced by earthworms (Eisenia fetida — Red Wiggler) digesting cow dung and organic matter. The earthworm gut transforms raw cow dung into nutrient-rich castings with 5x more nitrogen, 7x more phosphorus, and 11x more potassium than the original material. Setup: Construct a vermicompost bed (10ft x 4ft x 2ft) in shade. Layer: bottom — coconut coir or dried leaves (bedding), middle — partially decomposed cow dung (aged 15-20 days), top — introduce 1-2 kg Eisenia fetida worms per bed. Maintain moisture at 60-70% (squeeze test: should hold together but not drip). Temperature: 20-30°C. Harvest after 45-60 days when material is dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling. One bed produces 500-700 kg vermicompost per cycle. Application: 2-3 tonnes per acre mixed into soil before sowing.
source_doc: ICAR - Indian Institute of Soil Science, Bhopal

content: Mulching with Gliricidia (Gliricidia sepium) — known locally as 'Girigidia' in Karnataka — is one of the most effective green leaf manure practices for improving soil organic matter. Gliricidia is a fast-growing leguminous tree that fixes atmospheric nitrogen through Rhizobium bacteria in root nodules. It produces 8-10 tonnes of green biomass per hectare per year when planted as a hedge along field borders. Practice: Plant Gliricidia stakes (1.5 ft long, pencil-thick) along field borders at 1 metre spacing. After 6-8 months, lop branches at 1 metre height. Spread cut leaves and tender branches directly on the soil surface between crop rows as mulch. The leaves decompose within 15-20 days, releasing nitrogen (2.5-3% N), phosphorus (0.2% P), and potassium (2% K). Lopping can be done 3-4 times per year. The C:N ratio of Gliricidia leaves is 12:1 — ideal for rapid decomposition.
source_doc: UAS Bangalore - Green Leaf Manure Practices for Karnataka

content: Nugge (Moringa oleifera / Drumstick tree) as Green Manure: Moringa leaves contain exceptionally high nutrient content — 2.3% nitrogen, 0.3% phosphorus, 1.5% potassium, plus significant calcium, magnesium, iron, and zinc. In Vruksha Ayurveda, Moringa is described as 'Shigru' and valued for its ability to improve soil when its leaves are incorporated. Practice: Grow Moringa trees along field boundaries or as scattered trees within the farm. Harvest leaves periodically (every 45-60 days during growing season). Spread fresh leaves on the soil surface as mulch at 2-3 tonnes per acre. The leaves decompose rapidly (7-10 days) due to their low C:N ratio. Additionally, Moringa leaf extract (1 kg leaves blended in 10L water, filtered) can be used as a foliar spray that provides natural cytokinins (zeatin) which promote cell division and crop growth.
source_doc: Vruksha Ayurveda - Surapala

content: Agase (Sesbania / Dhaincha — Sesbania rostrata) for Soil Nitrogen Fixation: Agase is a fast-growing leguminous green manure crop that can fix 80-120 kg nitrogen per hectare in just 45-60 days of growth. It is the most efficient nitrogen-fixing green manure crop for Karnataka's soil conditions. Practice: Sow Agase seeds at 25 kg per hectare as a sole crop during the pre-monsoon period (May-June). Allow to grow for 45-60 days until flowering stage. At this point, the biomass reaches 15-20 tonnes per hectare of green matter. Incorporate into soil using a disc plough or rotavator. Allow 15 days for decomposition before sowing the main crop. The incorporated biomass adds nitrogen equivalent to 60-80 kg of urea per acre — completely replacing chemical nitrogen fertiliser. Agase also improves soil structure, water-holding capacity, and provides habitat for beneficial soil organisms.
source_doc: UAS Dharwad - Green Manuring in Karnataka Agriculture

content: Soil Biology and Why Organic Matter Matters: Soil is not just mineral particles — it is a living ecosystem. One teaspoon of healthy soil contains: 1 billion bacteria, 1 million fungi, 10,000 protozoa, and 10,000 nematodes. These organisms form the Soil Food Web: (1) Bacteria and fungi decompose organic matter and release nutrients, (2) Protozoa eat bacteria and release nitrogen in plant-available ammonium form, (3) Nematodes and arthropods regulate bacterial/fungal populations, (4) Earthworms aerate soil and create channels for water and root penetration. Chemical fertilisers (urea, DAP) bypass this natural system, directly injecting salts that kill soil organisms. Over time, chemically-treated soil becomes biologically dead — requiring ever-increasing doses of fertiliser. Organic inputs like cow dung, mulch, and Jeevamrutha feed and sustain the Soil Food Web, building long-term fertility.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: The Four Wheels of ZBNF (Subhash Palekar's Zero Budget Natural Farming): The ZBNF system rests on four foundational practices, all derived from indigenous cow breeds: (1) JEEVAMRUTHA — fermented microbial culture applied to soil every 15 days to maintain soil biological activity, (2) BIJAMRUTHA — seed treatment before sowing to protect from soil-borne disease, (3) MULCHING (Acchadana) — three types: straw mulch (surface cover), live mulch (cover crops), and soil mulch (tillage-created dust layer to prevent moisture evaporation), (4) WAAPHASA — maintaining optimal soil moisture (50% air, 50% water in soil pores). When all four wheels work together, external inputs become unnecessary. The cow provides the biology (dung and urine for Jeevamrutha/Bijamrutha), mulching provides the physical structure, and Waaphasa maintains the moisture balance. Cost: virtually zero compared to Rs. 5,000-15,000 per acre for chemical inputs.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: Straw Mulching (Acchadana) in ZBNF: After harvesting any crop, the leftover stubble and straw should NEVER be burnt. Instead, spread it evenly on the soil surface as mulch. Benefits: (1) Prevents moisture evaporation — reduces irrigation requirement by 30-50%, (2) Suppresses weed growth — blocks sunlight from reaching weed seeds, (3) Maintains soil temperature — keeps root zone cool in summer, warm in winter, (4) Feeds soil organisms — earthworms and fungi break down straw into humus, (5) Prevents soil erosion during heavy rains. Application: Spread crop residue 3-4 inches thick on the soil surface. Do not incorporate. For paddy straw, spread between rows of the next crop. For sugarcane trash, use as mulch in the same field. In Karnataka's Southern Dry Zone, straw mulching is critical during October-February when moisture stress is highest. One acre of paddy produces 2-3 tonnes of straw — sufficient to mulch the same acre.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: Why Indian Cow Breeds (Bos indicus) are Superior for Organic Farming: Indian cow breeds like Gir, Sahiwal, Red Sindhi, Hallikar, Amrit Mahal, and Malnad Gidda have a fundamentally different gut microbiome compared to foreign breeds (Holstein, Jersey). Indian cow dung contains 300-500 billion beneficial bacteria per gram, compared to 3-5 billion in exotic breed dung. Key differences: (1) Indian cow dung has higher populations of cellulolytic bacteria — critical for decomposing crop residue in soil, (2) Indian cow urine has higher allantoin content — a natural root growth promoter, (3) The A2 milk protein in Indian breeds is also reflected in higher quality urine and dung. For Jeevamrutha and all ZBNF preparations, Subhash Palekar specifically recommends using LOCAL desi cow dung and urine. Even one local cow can provide sufficient dung and urine for treating 30 acres of farmland using ZBNF methods.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: Dashaparni Ark — Ten-Leaf Extract for Organic Pest Management: Dashaparni Ark is a botanical pest repellent made from ten types of leaves. Ingredients: Neem leaves (5 kg), Calotropis/Ekka (2 kg), Karanj/Pongamia (2 kg), Custard apple leaves (2 kg), Sitaphal seeds (500g crushed), Datura leaves (500g), Tobacco waste (500g), Green chilli paste (500g), Garlic paste (250g), Cow urine (10 litres), and 200 litres water. Preparation: Crush and mix all leaves, add cow urine, fill with water in a barrel. Cover and ferment for 30 days. Stir every 3 days. After 30 days, filter through cloth. Application: Dilute 1 litre of Dashaparni Ark in 100 litres of water. Spray on affected crops in the evening (UV light degrades some active compounds). Effective against: aphids, whiteflies, stem borers, fruit borers, and mites. The combination of azadirachtin (neem), nicotine (tobacco), capsaicin (chilli), and allicin (garlic) provides broad-spectrum pest repellent activity.
source_doc: Subhash Palekar - Zero Budget Natural Farming

content: Vruksha Ayurveda — The Ancient Indian Science of Plant Health: Vruksha Ayurveda, authored by Surapala (circa 1000 CE), is one of the oldest known treatises on plant science and agriculture. Key principles relevant to modern organic farming: (1) SOIL CLASSIFICATION — Surapala classified soils by colour, taste, smell, and texture, correlating with fertility levels. Black soil (Krishna Mritika) = highest fertility, suitable for most crops. Red soil (Rakta Mritika) = needs organic amendment. Sandy soil (Sikata) = needs heavy mulching. (2) PLANT NUTRITION — described as 'Vriksha Ahara' (plant food), recommending organic manures, bone meal, oil cakes, and specific herbal preparations. (3) PLANT DISEASES — described as imbalances of Vata, Pitta, and Kapha in plants, treated with herbal formulations. (4) SEED TREATMENT — seeds soaked in milk, honey, and specific plant extracts before sowing. Many practices in Vruksha Ayurveda align with modern soil science and plant pathology.
source_doc: Vruksha Ayurveda - Surapala (translated by Nalini Sadhale)

content: Chemical Fertiliser vs Organic: The Economic and Ecological Reality for Indian Farmers: India imports approximately 30% of its urea requirement. In 2023-24, India spent Rs. 1.28 lakh crore on fertiliser subsidies. A farmer using chemical inputs spends Rs. 8,000-15,000 per acre per season on fertilisers and pesticides alone. In contrast, ZBNF (Zero Budget Natural Farming) brings this external input cost to near zero. One desi cow provides enough dung and urine for 30 acres. India has 300 million cattle — if even 100 million are used for organic input production, India can completely eliminate chemical fertiliser dependency. Beyond economics, chemical fertiliser dependency creates: (1) Soil degradation — 30% of Indian agricultural soil is now classified as degraded, (2) Water pollution — nitrate contamination of groundwater in Punjab, Haryana, (3) Health costs — pesticide residues linked to cancer clusters in high-input farming regions, (4) Farmer debt — high input costs are a primary driver of farmer distress.
source_doc: NITI Aayog - Natural Farming Report 2023

content: Karnataka-Specific Agro-Zones and Organic Practices: Karnataka has 10 agro-climatic zones, each requiring adapted organic farming practices. Southern Dry Zone (Zone 6 — Bengaluru Rural, Tumkur, Mandya): Annual rainfall 600-800mm. Critical practice: straw mulching + Jeevamrutha. Drought-adapted crops: Ragi, millets, pulses. Apply Jeevamrutha every 15 days during kharif. Northern Dry Zone (Zone 3 — Dharwad, Belgaum, Hubli): Annual rainfall 500-700mm. Key practice: Gliricidia hedge + groundnut-sorghum rotation with vermicompost. Apply vermicompost at 3t/acre. Coastal Zone (Zone 10 — Dakshina Kannada, Uttara Kannada): High rainfall 3000-4000mm. Key practice: coconut husk mulching, Panchagavya spray for arecanut/pepper. Malnad Zone (Zone 9 — Shimoga, Chikmagalur): Coffee-cardamom-pepper region. Key practice: shade tree leaf mulch + Jeevamrutha for coffee estates. Southern Transition Zone (Zone 7 — Mysore, Hassan): Moderate rainfall. Key: green manuring with Sesbania before rice transplanting.
source_doc: UAS Bangalore - Agro-Climatic Zone Agriculture Guide

content: Ragi (Finger Millet) Organic Cultivation — Karnataka's Primary Millet: Ragi is the staple crop of Southern Karnataka, grown on 1.2 million hectares. Organic Ragi cultivation with ZBNF methods: (1) SEED TREATMENT: Soak ragi seeds in Bijamrutha for 20 minutes before nursery sowing. (2) NURSERY: Raised beds with 2 inches of well-decomposed vermicompost. Transplant at 21-25 days. (3) MAIN FIELD: Apply Jeevamrutha at 200L/acre before transplanting. Apply again every 15 days for first 60 days. (4) MULCHING: After 30 days, apply straw mulch or Gliricidia leaf mulch between rows. (5) PEST MANAGEMENT: For army worm (ಸೈನ್ಯ ಹುಳು), spray Dashaparni Ark at 1% concentration. For blast disease, spray Panchagavya at 3% concentration preventively. (6) YIELD: Organic ragi yields 12-15 quintals/acre with ZBNF methods, comparable to conventional yields of 14-16 quintals/acre but at zero input cost.
source_doc: UAS Bangalore - Organic Ragi Production Technology

content: Soil Testing and Understanding Your Soil Without a Laboratory: Farmers can assess basic soil health using traditional methods described in Vruksha Ayurveda and validated by modern soil science. (1) WATER PERCOLATION TEST: Dig a 30cm hole. Fill with water. If water drains within 30 minutes = sandy, well-drained. 30-120 minutes = loam, ideal. Over 120 minutes = clay, needs organic matter. (2) EARTHWORM COUNT: Dig a 30cm cube of soil. Count earthworms. Less than 3 = poor soil biology (chemically damaged). 3-10 = moderate. More than 10 = healthy soil. (3) SMELL TEST: Healthy soil smells earthy and pleasant (geosmin produced by Streptomyces bacteria). Chemical/sour smell = anaerobic conditions, needs aeration. (4) ROOT TEST: Pull up a weed. Healthy soil shows extensive root branching. Poor soil shows stunted, shallow roots. (5) COLOUR: Darker soil = more organic matter = more fertile. If your soil scores poorly on these tests, begin with heavy Jeevamrutha application (every 7 days instead of 15) for the first season.
source_doc: Vruksha Ayurveda - Surapala / Palekar ZBNF combined
